text: Surely he's been invited by now :thinking_face:. I figure he might prefer to work in isolation? :shrug:
Bret isn't known for attending FoC workshops either afaik
But then he has a very unique perspective on what a humane future looks like, so that makes sense. For Bret, it's not about better spreadsheets.

To me it sounds like old and smart guys talking about Smalltalk + its environment, lisp + emacs (or + Cursive) or a bit like C# and the Unity3d Editor. Oh and let me add React-live here :slightly_smiling_face: <https://github.com/FormidableLabs/react-live>

text: Welcome Sam! What is a diagrammatic proof tool? Can you share more of what you are working on in your research?

Oh, MPS and Smalltalk are definitely in the same (or similar) vein for my overall goals!

The demo I'm looking to make is just to make a tangible point or analogy about artificial boundaries, but definitely doesn't offer a solution for fluidly dissolving that boundary at will (unless you count manually rewriting code and manually implementing extra UIs and parsers, etc., for a one-time use scenario, which is not exactly practical).

Those tools probably offer the highest degree of fluidity currently available (shout out to Glamorous Toolkit, too!), but what they don't do is make the live code (or equivalent) for the tool itself present and editable in itself. It's hard to explain in few words what that gains, but I think it's key to making truly moldable software in every sense.

It's hard to explain in few words what that gains, but I think it's key to making truly moldable software in every sense.
Really interesting! :slightly_smiling_face:
Really interesting! :slightly_smiling_face:
<@UK69SS8LB> Are you familiar with standard proof tools such as isabelle and coq?
I'm familiar with coq. Not isabelle though.
Ok, the general idea of the tool I'm working on is the same, but the proofs are expressed through diagrams rather than textual algebra.
It's very much a research tool and is extremely limited in actual use (it can only work with proofs about natural numbers).
<https://www.aaai.org/Papers/Symposia/Fall/1997/FS-97-03/FS97-03-007.pdf> this paper talks about the tool better than I can probably.

My term has yet to start, so I haven't actually begun work yet, but my proposal was to add automated theorem proving to the tool (this will likely involve a rewrite, as it dates from the late 90s, and afaik hasn't been touched much since).

I explained a bit if this in this other thread:

<https://futureofcoding.slack.com/archives/C5U3SEW6A/p1569829071119800?thread_ts=1569596004.115400&cid=C5U3SEW6A>

attachments.fallback:
[September 30th, 2019 12:37 AM] dcook0819: I agree that's not the best place to end up, and the idea is actually to find / implement a more ideal representation on top of it -- probably something static or declarative -- and then the layer beneath it transforms it into the "real thing".

That way the thing being specified / coded is 1st or 0 level -- and perhaps the best form reasonably possible for that thing -- and the 2nd level stuff acts more like a compiler or interpreter, which is 2nd level by definition.

The benefit is that you break free of restrictions imposed by any one language or format or editor, because you specify it exactly how you want.

This also blurs the lines between programming language, compiler/interpreter, user interaction, macros, and functions + data structures. Really these are all these same thing: specification, and the thing that builds / transforms it. The only difference is when & where that happens. Really it's all just functions + data, so my idea is to just put it all in the same place and compose / separate / invoke the different pieces in the right order.

text: Ah got it. I was thinking of commutative diagrams. It looks like it's algebra represented in diagrams. I have not been exposed to this area. It'd be great to hear from you as your term progresses!
